cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure this out. The question is about a passage that discusses the ownership of the "Happy Birthday to You" song. The user wants to know what the history experts' statement proves about the song. First, I need to look at the passage again. The main points are: - Filmmaker Jennifer Nelson had to pay $1500 to use the song in her documentary about its history. - Warner Music Group claims copyright since 1988. - Nelson's lawyers argue that the song belongs to the public because of its history. - The song was originally written by the Hill sisters as "Good Morning to All" in 1893. - Over time, people started using the "Happy Birthday" lyrics to the same tune. - History experts say there's no record of who wrote the lyrics, and the public was singing it long before Warner claimed copyright. - The lawyers want Warner to return fees collected over four years. The question is asking what the history experts' statement proves. The options are: A) does not belong to Warner Music Group B) has more than 200 years' history C) was initially owned by another company D) has always been very popular Now, let's analyze each option. Option B says the song has more than 200 years of history. The song was written in 1893, which is about 125 years ago (as of 2023), so that's not 200 years. So B is incorrect. Option C suggests it was initially owned by another company. The passage says the sisters wrote "Good Morning to All," but there's no mention of a company owning it initially. The current copyright claim by Warner started in 1988, but there's no info here about another company before that, so C is probably not correct. Option D says it has always been very popular. While the song is well-known, the passage doesn't specifically state that it has "always been very popular." The focus is on ownership, not popularity, so D might not be the answer. Option A says the song does not belong to Warner Music Group. The experts' statements point out that the song's origin is unclear, that the public was using it long before Warner claimed it, and that there's no record of the original lyric writers. This supports the argument
